text: Hello everyone. So today we're going to talk about how to do a difficult laparoscopic appendectomy.

Now this was a burst appendix with lump formation which we went ahead with an interval appendectomy. I was assisted by Dr. Vipul Bothra. So this was a fifteen year old boy who was initially admitted with pain in abdomen and then was detected to a lump and we deferred his appendectomy.

The laparoscopic view reveals significant lump formation with adhesions. The omentum is visibly adhered to the surrounding structures, including the cecum, which is stuck to the lateral abdominal wall.

Why that is significant is not because of the physiology, but because of the fact that the red color of the blood absorbs a lot of light. So immediately as you get that, you're going to have a little drop in your illumination of the field. So if you're not already at a larger illumination and if you think that there is still scope to increase illumination in your light source, please do that because that is something that goes out of our mind when you start operating on such a difficult case and then you realize that, you know, somehow your vision is not good.
The surgeon carefully removes the omentum from the lateral abdominal wall, revealing the ileocecal junction adhered to the wall.

The surgeon performs the Cheetle's maneuver, medializing the cecum by detaching it from the lateral abdominal wall. This allows the cecum to be flipped medially, providing access to the appendix from the lateral side.

A lot of these burst appendixes happen because the things get localized very fast and the patient doesn't develop a lot of symptoms. Now why the things get localized very fast is only if the appendix is retrocecal or the appendix is pelvic or the appendix is paracolic in the right paracolic gutter. So the best approach in these cases is to go laterally. You approach it from the lateral side, lift up the cecum. It's easy to identify it. And if need be, you can actually strip off the peritoneum to avoid serosal tears.

The surgeon has localized the appendix amidst significant adhesions. The surgical field shows the appendix surrounded by dense tissue adhesions, which require careful and patient dissection to separate.

The surgeon carefully separates the omentum strands to expose the burst tip of the appendix, which is buried within the omental tissue. The process is slow and meticulous to avoid bleeding, as the omentum is known to bleed heavily. The laparoscopic approach minimizes the risk of wound infection by keeping the port of access distant from the surgical field.

The surgeon carefully examines the attachments to the appendix, while managing smoke and fogging in the laparoscopic field. The cecum has been medialized, providing ample space for dissection. The surgeon uses both hands to achieve the optimal angle for dissection, ensuring instruments do not cross.

The surgeon holds the appendix while observing that the terminal ileum, just before the ileocecal junction, is adhered to the appendix. This site of perforation has caused the ileum and omentum to form a large lump. The surgeon carefully maneuvers instruments from the left side to avoid crossing them and obstructing the view.
The surgeon carefully strips the serosa from the appendix while preserving the serosa of the ileum, avoiding serosal tears. The approach is from the lateral abdominal wall to minimize damage.

That in case things are stuck very badly, you can strip off the peritoneum of the lateral abdominal wall and not really strip off the serosa of the cecum or the ascending colon in the process of doing a desiolysis. Be diligent, try to use cautery to a minimum where only you're anticipating bleeding or you're anticipating that, you know, I need to, I can cut this safely. Now, these are the cases in laparoscopy especially where you can have the potential of collateral damage. You know, you may end up damaging something which you don't want to damage invariably. So in these cases, you have to stick to your basic surgical principles.

Keep the instrument in vision all the time. Make sure all the structures are free from the instrument and they're not touching anywhere and there's no current coupling effect which is happening. So keep that in mind. Be diligent. Be slow. Keep reassessing where you are. You know, change your grip. Look where the base of the appendix is. Where is the tip of the appendix? How close is the ileum? Do I need to dissect any further or can I stop my dissection here?

Do bare minimum that is essential to get rid of the appendix. Trust me, whatever post-op adhesions that you will get, post-op adhesive obstruction that patients land in is usually due to over-enthusiastic dissection, which creates a lot of raw surface on the structures that you're going to leave behind. You're not going to remove the cecum. You're not going to remove the ileum. The more you dissect these structures, the more damage you cause to these structures in the form of serosal tears or any kind of burns, the more the chances of post-operative adhesions. So stick close to the appendix. See the amount of bleeding which has happened. Now it may look a lot of bleeding on laparoscopy, but that was barely about twenty ml of bleeding that you got.

The surgeon performs a thorough wash of the surgical field to clear blood, enhancing visibility. The omentum, cecum, and ileum are carefully reassessed for bleeding, serosal tears, or perforations before proceeding with the appendectomy.

The surgeon applies a transfixation suture to secure the base of the appendix, ensuring stability and preventing slippage or sloughing off. This technique is used in cases of acute appendicitis without the use of an endolube.

Maybe one in a hundred patient may have a bad basal perforation where I may need to suture the cecum. In those cases, this practice will help me to make sure that I don't convert it to open just for that one single reason. Secondly, what it does is it improves my intracorporeal suturing technique. Now, appendix is the commonest surgery that you're going to do laparoscopy. whether it's in the beginning of your laparoscopic career or whether it is later in your laparoscopic career. That is the commonest surgery you're going to do. The next commonest after that is usually a hernia or a piloplasty. Now, I don't do hernias, laparoscopic. So for me, next is piloplasty. Piloplasty involves extensive suturing. So how do you bridge this gap? You bridge this gap by transfixing every appendix. Irrespective of whether it is acute, whether it is an elective appendectomy, whether there is a basal perforation or not, I choose to do a transfixation. It improves and gives me real-time practice of my intracorporeal knotting and suturing technique and refines my techniques for more complex procedures subsequently that I am going to do once I am confident of laparoscopic skills.

The surgeon prepares to remove the appendix specimen, which is visibly inflamed and previously perforated, by placing it into an endo bag for extraction. Improved illumination in the surgical field is noted after clearing blood from the area.
